titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Stone Buddha Valley

div.p: 徂徠山陰邃谷間，有古石佛，鄉人呼為石佛峪。其址於泰安市岱岳區化馬灣鄉紅山村。
峪中破山壁為龕（），中奉一佛二菩薩之像（）。其佛也，圓面肉髻，面相圓秀，著對襟衣，趺坐須彌臺。雙手交叉於腹前，似作“禪定印”。兩側脅侍菩薩，合掌待立，皆低眉垂首，呈虔敬之態（中像高57、寛33厘米，左右像高50、寛12—13厘米）。三像共龕而祀。其工則簡潔樸實，龕像比例略失調，應出諸民間工匠手。龕外罩鑿楹石檐以護，前幷有石几，似皆為後世所益者。
龕内西壁獲刻文4行（高32厘米、寛14厘米；），皆貝葉中偈語也。字作魏體，或亦北朝人鐫。峪去光化諸寺不遠，武平年映佛岩、將軍石者刻經皆萃於此，靑山雲谷，盡化佛國，蓋當日祇洹環匝之區也。則此經像悉出於東魏、高齊，殆無可疑。

figure.head: 石佛峪佛龕外景。2023年拍攝
Overview of the image niche at Stone Buddha Valley in Hongshan Village; photograph taken in 2023

div.head: 《大般涅槃經》
div.p: 石佛峪刻銘包含兩則偈頌，皆選自《大般涅槃經》。
第一則偈頌爲四言四句偈，即著名的《諸行無常偈》，亦稱《雪山偈》。此偈宣講娑婆世間無常，有生即有滅；衆生衹有終止這生滅的循環，纔能到達涅槃常樂境界：

lg.l: 諸行無常， 是生滅法。
生滅滅巳， 寂滅爲樂。此偈見於《大般涅槃經》的三個漢文譯本：《大般涅槃經》T#7，1：204c23－24。《大般涅槃經》T#374，12：450a16，451a1。《大般涅槃經》T#375，12：692a13，693a1。有關此偈起源的簡要概述以及更多的參考文獻，參見《中國佛教石經·山東省第三卷》第34頁；何書群第535－542頁。

div.p:
《大般涅槃經》中講述，釋迦牟尼佛於過去世爲雪山童子時，由羅剎口中聽聞前二句，不惜生命欲獲得全偈，許下誓言： 然後處處，若石、若壁、若樹、若道，書寫此偈。 《大般涅槃經》T#374，12：452a5－6。 。 虔誠的佛教信徒遵循此誓言，在摩崖和碑刻上留下了石刻例證：
高昌小型石塔，5世紀上半葉，現藏柏林洪堡論壇亞洲藝術博物館（）。
四川省茂縣壩中村寨《無量壽佛像碑》，紀年南齊永明元年（483），現藏四川博物院。圖版參見《中國佛教石經·山東省第三卷》第48頁，圖17。
山東省濟南歷城黄石崖（黄石崖1號），北魏孝昌二年（526）左右；有關該石刻的時代，參見《中國佛教石經·山東省第三卷》第158－160頁。
山東省司里山1號，北齊皇建二年（561）之前；有關該石刻的時代，參見《中國佛教石經·山東省第一卷》第391頁、第394頁和第408－414頁。
河南省安陽小南海中窟，天保六年至皇建元年（555－560）間；有關小南海中窟該刻銘的内容分析以及更多的參考文獻，參見何書群第535－542頁。
河北省涉縣木井寺《觀世音經碑》（木井寺1號），北齊武平二年（571），現藏媧皇宮北齊石刻陳列館。
山東省尖山支鍋石（尖山16.1號），北齊武平六年（575）左右。見《中國佛教石經·山東省第三卷》第34頁和第128－134頁。
河北省南響堂山6號洞窟的窟楣，北齊（550－577）。拓本見張林堂和許培蘭第32頁。
第二則偈頌爲五言四句偈，宣説佛陀已證得上偈之法，强調衹要有人全心聽聞，即同樣能獲得常樂涅槃：

lg.l: 如來證涅槃，永斷於生死。
若有至心聽，常得無量樂。《大般涅槃經》T#374，12：497b9－10。《大般涅槃經》T#375，12：740c27－28。英譯見何書群第544頁。

div.p: 此偈亦見於《大般涅槃經》中的一個本生故事：釋迦牟尼佛的一個前身曾是一個熱衷追求佛法但極其貧窮的人，因身上無半分錢可供養當時的佛陀（亦稱釋迦牟尼），即將自己賣身給一需要吃人肉養疾的病人。此富裕病人先給予求法心切的貧人一日三兩肉的代價金錢五枚，并准許他放假一日前往禮拜佛陀。貧人立馬將全數金錢供養佛陀，并悉心聆聽佛陀宣説《大般涅槃經》。資質愚鈍的他僅能記住其中的五言四句偈，回到富人家後，在履行其義務的同時，全心受持偈頌。一個月之後，每日食三兩肉的富人病愈，而貧人的身體亦因受持該偈頌而完好如初。貧人見僅僅受持一偈的功效如此殊勝，即發菩提心，并誓願未來成佛。《大般涅槃經》T#374，12：497a20－b19。《大般涅槃經》T#375，12：740c7－741a9。

刊刻該偈頌於石佛峪的修行者，一定熟悉這個賣身求法的本生故事，并有同樣誓願成佛的決心。在他處，亦有修行者刊刻該偈頌於石，以表彰其心：
濟南歷城黄石崖（黄石崖1號），北魏孝昌二年（526）左右；有關該石刻的時代，參見《中國佛教石經·山東省第三卷》第158－160頁。
安陽小南海中窟，天保六年至皇建元年（555－560）間。有關小南海中窟該刻銘的内容分析以及更多的參考文獻，參見何書群第544－547頁。

figure.head: 石佛峪佛龕近景。2023年拍攝
The image niche at Stone Buddha Valley in Hongshan Village; photograph taken in 2023

div.head: Overview
div.p: On the northern slope of Mount Culai 徂徠山 in Hongshan Village 紅山村, Huamawan Township 化馬灣鄉, within the Daiyue District 岱岳區, Tai’an City 泰安市, is a secluded valley, called by locals Stone Buddha Valley (Shifo Yu 石佛峪 ).

In the valley, a niche has been carved directly into the mountain cliff (fig. ), enshrining a central Buddha flanked by two Bodhisattvas (fig. ). The Buddha features a rounded face and a cranial protuberance ( uṣṇīṣa , rouji 肉髻), with a serene and elegant countenance. He wears a traditional robe with a central opening and sits cross-legged on a Sumeru (Xumi 須彌) pedestal. His hands are crossed in front of his abdomen, seemingly in the Meditation mudrā ( dhyāna mudrā , chanding yin 禪定印). On either side, the attendant Bodhisattvas stand with palms pressed together, eyes cast down and heads bowed in a posture of deep reverence. (The central figure is 57 cm high and 33 cm wide; the flanking figures are 50 cm high and 12–13 cm wide).

These three icons are enshrined together in a single niche. The craftsmanship is simple and plainly adorned; the proportions between the figures and the niche are slightly misaligned, suggesting they were produced by local folk artisans. The exterior of the niche is sheltered by a stone eave and pillars carved from the rock, and a stone table sits before it—all of which appear to be later additions.

On the western wall inside the niche, four columns of text are inscribed (32 cm high, 14 cm wide; fig. ), consisting of verses ( gāthā , ji 偈) from Buddhist scriptures. The valley is not far from the Guang­hua Monastery 光化寺. During the wuping 武平 era (570–576) of the Northern Qi dynasty, the stone-cut sutras of Radiant Buddha Cliff (Yingfo Yan 映佛岩) and General Rock (Jiangjun Shi 將軍石) were carved near this monastery. Amidst the green mountains and cloudy valleys, the entire landscape was transformed into a Buddhist realm—truly a sacred precinct where monks once gathered and resided.

div.head: Inscription
div.p: The stone carvings at Stone Buddha Valley contain two verses, both selected from the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra (Da boniepan jing 大般涅槃經).

The first verse contains four lines of four characters each and is the famous Verse of Impermanence of all Conditioned Phenomena (Zhu xing wu chang ji 諸行無常偈), also known as the Snow Mountain Verse (Xue Shan ji 雪山偈). This verse preaches the impermanence of the Sahā (suopo 娑婆) world, where whatever is born must perish; sentient beings can only reach the realm of eternal joy of Nirvana (niepan 涅槃) by terminating this cycle of birth and death:

lg.l: this is the Law of birth and extinction.
After birth and extinction have been extinguished,

l.foreign: 諸行無常， 是生滅法。
生滅滅巳， 寂滅爲樂。
l.note: T#7, 1: 204c23–24; T#374, 12: 450a16, 451a1; T#375, 12: 692a13, 693a1. For a brief overview of the origin of this verse and further references, see 48–49; 535–542.

div.p: The Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra recounts that when Shakyamuni Buddha (Shijiamouni Fo 釋迦牟尼佛) was the Snow Mountain Boy (Xue shan tongzi 雪山童子) in a past life, he heard the first two lines from the mouth of a rākṣasa (luocha 羅剎). Willing to sacrifice his life to obtain the full verse, he made a vow:
div.quote: Thereafter, everywhere, whether on stones, walls, trees, or roads, I shall write this verse.T#374, 12:452a5-6.
div.p:
Devout Buddhist followers honored this vow, inscribing the verse on cliffs and steles:
Miniature stupa from Gaochang, first half of the 5th century, currently housed in the Museum of Asian Art, Humboldt Forum Berlin (fig. );
The Image Stele of Buddha Amitāyus
無量壽佛像碑 unearthed at Bazhong Village Stronghold 壩中村寨 in Mao County 茂縣, Sichuan, dated to 483, currently housed in the Sichuan Museum; For an image of the inscription, see 48, fig. 17.
HSY 1 at Yellow Stone Cliff (Huangshiya 黄石崖) in Licheng 歷城, Jinan 濟南, Shandong, around 526;Regarding the dating of this stone inscription, see 158–160.
Sili 1 at Mount Sili 司里山, Shandong, prior to 561 ; Regarding the dating of this stone inscription, see 401–403, 407–414.
The Middle Cave of Xiao Nanhai 小南海 in Anyang 安陽, Henan, between 555 and 560;For an analysis of the inscription content and further references, see 535–542.
Stele of the Sutra on the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin Jing 觀世音經) from the Mujing Monastery 木井寺 (MJS 1), Shexian 涉縣, Hebei, 571, currently housed in the Exhibition Hall of Stone Carvings from the Northern Qi at the Palace of Empress Wa (Wahuang Gong Bei Qi shike chenlieguan 媧皇宫北齊石刻陳列館);
JS 16.1 at “Pot Stand Rocks” (Zhiguo shi 支鍋石) at Mount Jian 尖山, Shandong, around 575;See 48–49 and 128–134.
The lintel of Cave 6 at at Southern Mount Xiangtang (Nan Xiangtang Shan 南響堂山), Hebei, the Northern Qi 北齊 dynasty (550–577).For a rubbing see 32.

The second verse contains four lines of five characters each, declaring that the Buddha has already attained the dharma of the previous verse, emphasizing that as long as one listens with a sincere heart, they can equally attain the eternal bliss of Nirvana:

lg.l: and cuts off [once and for all the cycle of] birth-and-death (saṃsāra).
If there is [one who] listens with all their heart,
they will eternally enjoy limitless bliss (sukha).Modified translation from 544.

l.foreign: 如來證涅槃，永斷於生死。
若有至心聽，常得無量樂。

div.p: This verse is also imbeded in a past-life story ( jātaka ; benshen gushi 本生故事) within the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra: One of the past incarnations of Shakyamuni Buddha was an extremely poor man deeply passionate about pursuing the Buddha Dharma. Having not a single penny to offer the then-present Buddha who was also named Shakyamuni, he was willing to sell himself to a sick man who needed to eat human flesh to cure his illness. This wealthy patient first gave the eager truth-seeking poor man five coins as the price for three gloss of flesh per day, and granted him a one-day leave to go and worship the Buddha. The poor man immediately offered all the money to the Buddha and attentively listened to the Buddha preach the Great Parinirvāṇa Sutra. Being of dull aptitude, he could only remember the verse in concern within the sutra. After returning to the wealthy man's house, while fulfilling his obligations, he wholeheartedly received and upheld (shouchi 受持) the verse. One month later, the wealthy man who ate three taels of flesh daily recovered, and the poor man's body was also completely restored to its original state due to upholding the verse. Seeing that the efficacy of upholding just a single verse was so extraordinary, the poor man generated a heart of awakening (Bodhicitta; puti xin 菩提心) and vowed to become a Buddha in the future.T#374, 12:497a20-b19.T#375, 12:740c7-741a9.

figure.head: 紅山村石佛峪刻經位置示意圖。2023年拍攝
Location of sutra carving in the image niche at Stone Buddha Valley in Hongshan Village; photograph taken in 2023
